title: From flat to narrow bands: Engineering quantum emission in a one-dimensional Lieb lattice
authors: Zhiyong Liu, Yue Sun, Ying Hu
abstract: We develop a comprehensive theoretical framework that unifies quantum emission dynamics in one-dimensional Lieb lattices, bridging the gap between ideal flat-band coherence and realistic narrow-band dissipation. By coupling an emitter to sublattices with finite flat-band wavefunction overlap, we activate a collective, size-independent interaction fundamentally distinct from dispersive-band processes. Controllably breaking lattice symmetry transforms the flat band into a narrow dispersive band, enabling a continuous crossover from non-Markovian to Markovian dynamics governed by the competition between coupling strength and engineered bandwidth. Crucially, we derive explicit scaling laws that provide a quantitative blueprint for tuning spontaneous emission from coherent trapping to Markovian decay. Our work provides a unified framework that connects idealized flat-band physics to emerging narrow-band platforms such as moir$\rm\acute{e}$ photonic crystals, offering a practical toolkit for interpreting experiments and engineering quantum emission in structured photonic environments.
summary: This paper develops a framework to understand how transitioning from a perfectly flat band to a narrow dispersive band affects quantum emission. It provides quantitative scaling laws to tune the system between coherent population trapping and Markovian decay. This is particularly relevant for engineering light-matter interactions in structured photonic platforms.
main_problem: Bridging the gap between idealized flat-band coherence and realistic narrow-band dissipation in quantum emission, specifically understanding how the transition from a dispersionless to a dispersive band alters light-matter coupling.
main_result: The authors derive scaling laws for the crossover from non-Markovian population trapping to Markovian decay, showing that the decay rate depends on the competition between coupling strength and engineered bandwidth.
method: The study uses a Green's function formalism with Lehmann spectral representation, self-energy analysis, and direct numerical simulations of the excited-state population.
model_or_system: A single quantum emitter coupled to a one-dimensional Lieb lattice consisting of three sublattices (A, B, and C), where an on-site potential on the C sublattice can be used to break symmetry and create a narrow dispersive band.
key_observables: Excited-state population P(t), steady-state population, spectral function A(omega), bound-state residues (Zs), and decay rate (Gamma).
important_parameters: Emitter-bath coupling strength (g), nearest-neighbor hopping (J), emitter detuning (Delta), and engineered bandwidth (W) or on-site potential (V0).
main_assumptions: The analysis utilizes a weak coupling limit (g << W << J) for certain regimes and assumes a rectangular spectral model to approximate transient dynamics.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the band structure transition from flat to narrow bands, the spectral function A(omega) showing bound states and continua, and the temporal evolution of the emitter population.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the 1D Lieb lattice model, analyzes sublattice-dependent coupling (B vs. C), explores the effect of detuning on emission dynamics, investigates the symmetry-breaking transition to narrow bands, and concludes with scaling laws for engineering quantum emission.
why_interesting: It provides a unified theoretical framework for controlling quantum emission dynamics, offering a toolkit for designing photonic environments like moire photonic crystals for applications in quantum memory or single-photon sources.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (Color online) Schematic, band structure, and flat- band wavefunctions of the 1D Lieb lattice. (a) Schematic il- lustration of a quantum emitter (blue sphere) locally coupled to the C sublattice of the 1D Lieb lattice model. (b) The corresponding band structure, featuring a central flat band (red line) at energy Ek = 0 and two dispersive bands (blue lines). (c) The spatial profile of the flat-band wavefunction in real space |ψj| for a lattice with N = 120 unit cells. The red, blue, and green bars represent the wavefunction amplitude on the A, B, and C sublattices, respectively, highlighting the compact localization and the dark-state nature of the B sub- lattice.

FIG. 2. (Color online) Spontaneous emission dynamics for an emitter coupled to the B sublattice with ∆= 0 and g = 0.4J. (a) Emitter population P(t) as a function of time. The pop- ulation saturates to a steady-state value P(t)|t→∞≈0.87, demonstrating strong population trapping. (b) The oscilla- tory component P(t) −|Ds(t)|2, quantifying the interference between bound states and the continuum.
FIG. 3. (Color online) Dynamics and spectral properties for an emitter coupled to the C sublattice with g = 0.4J. (a)-(c) Spontaneous emission dynamics P(t) (blue lines) for (a) ∆= 0, (b) ∆= √

FIG. 4. (Color online) Spontaneous emission dynamics in the modified 1D Lieb lattice with a narrow band. (a) Band structure of the modified model with V0 = 0.3J. The formerly flat band becomes a narrow dispersive band (red) with bandwidth W. Inset: Bandwidth W as a function of V0, showing the linear relationship W = αV0 with α ≈0.802. (b) Emitter population P(t) for different parameters, showing the transition from non-Markovian oscillations to Markovian decay under different bandwidth and coupling strength. The emitter detuning ∆is fixed at the center of the narrow band in all cases. (c) Total bound-state residue P
FIG. 5. (Color online) Dynamics and spectral properties for an emitter coupled to the A sublattice with g = 0.4J. (a)-(c) Spontaneous emission dynamics P(t) (blue lines) for (a) ∆= 0, (b) ∆= √
